title: New super rules spark $6bn small business cash crisis
full_text:
Australian businesses may need to find up to $6 billion in additional cash as new Payday Super rules loom, with experts warning small businesses could be forced to dip into personal savings to keep up with the changes.
The warning comes as the Federal Government prepares to introduce Payday Super from July 1, requiring employers to pay workers’ superannuation at the same time as wages rather than quarterly.
The Australian Taxation Office estimates around $6 billion in superannuation goes unpaid each year, with the reforms designed to close that gap and improve compliance.
ATO Deputy Commissioner Emma Rosenzweig said Payday Super would give the tax office earlier visibility of employers failing to meet their obligations.
“Payday Super will provide the ATO with earlier visibility of underpayment or non-payment of super, allowing for a proactive approach to compliance to reduce unpaid super. For those employers who are not paying super for their workers, this makes it harder to hide,” Ms Rosenzweig said.
“It’s important to make clear that for most employers, Payday Super won’t change the amount of super you are paying to your employees, just the payment frequency.”
Payday Super cheat sheet
But business groups and advisers warn the shift could intensify cashflow pressures, particularly for small businesses already struggling with late customer payments.
A survey of 500 small businesses by accounting software giant Xero found 87 per cent expect increased cashflow pressure under Payday Super, while one in three said they may need to draw on personal savings to meet the new obligations.
The research also found businesses lost an average of $15,257 over the past financial year due to customers paying invoices late.
Xero Executive General Manager of Strategy and Operations ANZ Charlie Sheppeard said business owners were less concerned about the policy itself than the reality of delayed customer payments.
“It’s not the rules they’re worried about, it’s the fact that their customers are paying them late,” Mr Sheppeard said.
“The Xero Small Business Insights data shows businesses are getting paid around a week late. When super has to go out every pay run, that’s a week’s worth of cash flow missing from the business and it creates pressure.”
Xero’s data showed small businesses were paid an average of 6.9 days late in the March quarter, up slightly from 6.7 days in the previous quarter.
H & R Block Director of Tax Communications Mark Chapman said industries operating on thin margins or uneven revenue streams could be hit hardest.
“Particularly businesses that have historically relied on the quarterly payment cycle to manage working capital. Industries such as hospitality, retail, construction and other sectors with tight margins or fluctuating revenue may feel the greatest impact,” Mr Chapman said.
He said businesses should recognise superannuation as an employee entitlement rather than a source of working capital and begin adjusting their financial planning now.
For businesses with irregular income patterns, the transition is expected to require significant changes to budgeting, payroll processes and cashflow forecasting.
Zimsen Partners Senior Accountant Joseph Ranasinghe said preparation would be critical to avoiding disruption.
Mr Ranasinghe said keeping bookkeeping up to date was “non-negotiable”, while treating superannuation like wages by setting funds aside every pay cycle would help smooth the transition.
He also encouraged businesses to review their payroll systems and processes now, saying the goal was not necessarily to spend more money on software but to ensure the right systems were in place to save time, improve compliance and reduce risk.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
